name: Senso-ji Temple
description: Tokyo's oldest and most visited Buddhist temple. Pass under the iconic Kaminarimon thundergate and explore the Nakamise shopping street before reaching the main hall.

name: Meiji Shrine
description: A serene Shinto shrine set within a forested 700,000 sq-metre park in the heart of the city, dedicated to Emperor Meiji and Empress Shoken.

name: Shinjuku Gyoen
description: One of Japan's finest gardens combining French formal, English landscape, and traditional Japanese styles. A must in cherry blossom season.

name: Shibuya Scramble Crossing
nameJa: 渋谷スクランブル交差点
description: The world's busiest intersection, with up to 3,000 pedestrians crossing at once. Grab a coffee above at the Starbucks or rooftop of Shibuya Sky for the full view.

name: Tsukiji Outer Market
description: The lively outer market that survived the 2018 tuna auction move. Dozens of stalls serve sushi, tamagoyaki, fresh seafood skewers, and street snacks.

name: Akihabara Electric Town
description: Neon-lit hub for electronics, manga, anime figures, retro games, and themed cafés. The undisputed capital of otaku culture.

name: teamLab Planets
nameJa: チームラボプラネッツ
description: A body-immersive art museum where you walk through water and are surrounded by infinite crystalline worlds, overgrown flower gardens, and koi projections.

name: Takeshita Street
description: Harajuku's pedestrian street packed with crepe stands, vintage boutiques, kawaii fashion shops, and Tokyo's most creative street style.

name: Nakameguro Canal Walk
description: A tree-lined canal flanked by indie cafés, concept stores, and galleries. Arguably Tokyo's most photographed neighbourhood — stunning in cherry blossom season.

name: Ueno Park & Museums
description: Tokyo's most famous park contains the Tokyo National Museum, National Museum of Western Art, a zoo, Shinobazu Pond, and dozens of cherry blossom trees.

name: Tokyo Skytree
description: The world's second-tallest structure at 634m. Two observation decks give panoramic views over greater Tokyo on clear days, with Fuji visible at sunrise.

name: Odaiba Waterfront
description: A futuristic artificial island featuring teamLab, Miraikan science museum, a life-size Gundam statue, onsen, and sweeping Rainbow Bridge views.

name: Yanaka Ginza
description: One of Tokyo's best-preserved old shitamachi (downtown) shopping streets. Browse craft shops, try fresh menchi katsu, and feel the Edo-era city that survived bombing.

name: Tokyo Station Ramen Street
nameJa: 東京ラーメンストリート
description: Eight celebrated ramen shops under Tokyo Station's Yaesu exit. Each serves a distinct regional style — from rich Sapporo miso to delicate Hakata tonkotsu.

name: Edo-Tokyo Museum
description: A dramatic elevated building housing full-scale reconstructions of Edo-period streetscapes, samurai culture, and the city's transformation from feudal town to megalopolis.

name: Shimokitazawa
description: Tokyo's indie heartland — narrow alleys full of vintage clothing, live music venues, curry shops, and the city's most creative young residents.

name: Ginza District
description: Tokyo's most prestigious shopping and dining district, with flagship stores from every luxury brand, Itoya stationery, and Mikimoto pearls since 1872.

name: Daikanyama T-Site
description: A stunning multi-building bookstore and lifestyle complex by architect Masao Hayakawa. Browse vinyl, art books, and concept stores in Tokyo's most photogenic neighbourhood.

name: Shinjuku Golden Gai
description: Six narrow alleyways containing ~200 tiny bars, each seating just 5–8 people. A legendary post-war drinking neighbourhood that survived bubble-era redevelopment.

name: Rikugien Garden
description: A classical Japanese stroll garden built in 1702, recreating 88 scenes from waka poetry. Famous for its weeping cherry tree illuminations each spring.

name: Ghibli Museum
nameJa: 三鷹の森ジブリ美術館
description: Studio Ghibli's whimsical museum in Mitaka, designed by director Hayao Miyazaki. Features original film cells, a rooftop robot soldier, a Catbus for children, and exclusive short films shown only here.

name: Nakano Broadway
description: A five-story shopping complex that has been the epicentre of manga, anime figures, and retro game collecting since the 1980s. Home to Mandarake's flagship store and dozens of specialist collectors' shops.

name: Tokyo National Museum
description: Japan's oldest and largest museum holds over 120,000 items — samurai armour, Buddhist sculpture, ukiyo-e prints, ancient pottery, and lacquerware. The 1938 Honkan building is a masterpiece of Imperial Crown Style architecture.

name: Tokyo Tower
description: Built in 1958 and inspired by the Eiffel Tower, Tokyo Tower remains a beloved icon at 333m. The Main Deck at 150m offers unobstructed city views, and on clear days Mount Fuji is visible on the horizon.

name: Omotesando
description: A wide zelkova-lined boulevard with flagship stores designed by Tadao Ando, Toyo Ito, and Herzog & de Meuron. Tokyo's premier architectural promenade, with excellent cafés in the side streets.

name: Ikebukuro & Sunshine City
nameJa: 池袋・サンシャインシティ
description: A mega-complex housing an aquarium, observatory, and Japan's largest concentration of anime merchandise stores outside Akihabara. The surrounding streets are packed with maid cafés, arcades, and manga shops.

name: Kabukicho
description: Japan's largest entertainment district — a grid of neon, arcades, karaoke towers, themed bars, and cinemas surrounding Toho's Godzilla-guarded building. Best experienced after dark.

name: Omoide Yokocho
description: A narrow post-war alley of yakitori stalls tucked beside Shinjuku Station's west exit, known as Memory Lane. Operating since the 1940s, it is most atmospheric after dark with smoke and lanterns.

name: Yoyogi Park
description: A vast urban forest adjacent to Meiji Shrine, popular for weekend festivals, open-air concerts, and picnics. The lawns are packed on Sundays with performers, cosplayers, and market vendors.

name: Hamarikyu Gardens
description: A 300-year-old tidal garden enclosed by Tokyo Bay and soaring skyscrapers. The seawater pond, island tea house, and duck blinds of a former shogunate retreat create a remarkable contrast with the city skyline.

name: Imperial Palace East Garden
description: The grounds of Edo Castle's innermost citadel, now a meticulously maintained public garden with original stone ramparts, moats, and seasonal plantings in the geographic heart of Tokyo.

name: Kagurazaka
description: A Franco-Japanese neighbourhood of cobbled alleys (yokocho), active geisha houses, French bistros, and refined kappo restaurants. One of Tokyo's most atmospheric and walkable dining districts.

name: Nezu Shrine
description: One of Tokyo's oldest shrines, with a miniature torii gate tunnel, an azalea garden that blazes pink in April, and a forested hill. Far less crowded than its Fushimi Inari counterpart.

name: Ryogoku Kokugikan
description: Japan's premier sumo arena, hosting three Grand Tournaments annually. Visit the sumo museum, eat chanko-nabe (wrestler's hot pot) at nearby stables, and observe morning practice sessions.

name: Jimbocho Book Town
description: The world's densest cluster of second-hand bookshops — over 170 dealers specialising in ukiyo-e prints, vintage manga, out-of-print academic texts, antique maps, and foreign-language books.

name: Shinjuku Ni-chome
description: One of Asia's largest LGBTQ+ neighbourhoods, with hundreds of intimate bars concentrated on a few compact blocks. Famously welcoming to all visitors and most festive on weekend evenings.

name: Koenji
description: Tokyo's vintage fashion capital — hundreds of second-hand boutiques, record shops, and indie live houses in a relaxed neighbourhood that resisted the city's redevelopment wave.

name: Kabuki-za Theatre
description: Japan's premier kabuki theatre in Ginza, rebuilt in 2013 with its iconic Momoyama-style façade preserved. Full programmes run multiple times daily — single-act tickets (hitomaku-mi) are available at the box office from around ¥1,000, no advance booking required.

name: Shibuya Sky
description: An open-air rooftop observation deck on the 46th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, 229 metres above the city. The unobstructed 360° views look directly down on the Scramble Crossing, with clear-day sightlines to Mount Fuji to the west.

name: Don Quijote Shinjuku
nameJa: ドン・キホーテ 新宿店
description: Tokyo's most famous outpost of Japan's legendary 24-hour discount mega-store, located in Kabukicho. Six floors of duty-free electronics, cosmetics, snacks, anime merchandise, costumes, and every quirky souvenir imaginable. Tax-free shopping for foreign visitors at the ground-floor counter.

name: teamLab Borderless
nameJa: チームラボボーダレス
description: A 10,000 sq-metre labyrinth of borderless digital art at Azabudai Hills — no maps, no fixed rooms, just immersive artwork that flows between spaces and reacts to your presence. The sister museum to teamLab Planets but on a far larger, more labyrinthine scale.

name: National Noh Theatre
description: Japan's premier government-run Noh stage in Sendagaya, hosting performances by the top schools of Noh and Kyogen. The building — with its traditional hinoki cypress stage and raked pine-tree backdrop — is itself a landmark of modern Japanese architecture. English programme notes are available.

name: Zojo-ji Temple
description: The official family temple of the Tokugawa shogunate, with six shoguns interred in its mausolea. The Great Main Hall frames a perfect view of Tokyo Tower rising behind it — one of the city's most iconic compositions — and the grounds hold a quiet row of 3,000 Jizo statues.
